### brookway17 — 2017-05-25T02:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-214615>

Sorry it's taken me so long to update my Journal, I have been extremely busy hacking my environment to be compliant with Dr. Kruse's protocols.

I removed all the fluorescent fixtures in my basement and replace those with recessed halogen. I always protect myself from blue light. I look like a weirdo when I go to the gym, I wear blue blocking clip ons over my glasses and long sleeve rashgard with a high neck and sweatpants and a cap. I also put mineral-based sunscreen on my hands and feet. I am only six months into a two-year membership at my gym. I haven't found any gyms in the area that are not lit with blue lights. I spend a lot of time outside, especially this time of year, busy doing lots of yard work. I try to do yoga on my patio whenever the weather cooperates.

On the EMF front I have reduced my wireless output by adding another wireless router that's located in the far corner of my basement next to the power panel. I am able to set the output power at 25% (cannot even pick up this router on my patio)and have it turned off at night time. I have run ethernet cable throughout the house, even my patio. I also got rid of my cordless phones. I wired phones around the house. I figured out how do you get ethernet on my iPad. There is one catch in the order to accomplish this you have to have The iPad hooked up to the charger. This generate some EMF In the electrical range (as measured by my trimeter). I figured out how to make a grounded power supply for the iPad. There is a ground point on the power supply inside the slide on plug assembly. I was able to access the ground Nub with an alligator clip and made an Interface to the power terminals using a power cord. It's not pretty but it works. I also build a faraday cage to sleep in. I used my son's Old bunk bed and wrapped aluminum screening around five Sides and silver mesh to allow access to the bed. My iPad cannot find any wireless networks inside the bed.

I installed a reverse Osmosis filter system with a remineralization cartridge.

I am eating a BAB every morning right after I watch the sunrise. I usually eat 3/4 of a pound of ground pork every morning with some sautéed vegetables from the night before. I cook the pork in the back fat that I cooked down the night before. I cook the pork while I am watching the sunrise. I have found lately that I can go all the way to 5 PM before getting hungry. At dinner I try to have seafood as many days a week as my wife will let me. I eat raw oysters whenever I go to Whole Foods. I am about seven weeks into the Leptin reset. I am too skinny and I hope to start gaining weight soon. I do high-intensity strength training three days a week. I have seen good gains in strength but I haven't put on a lot of muscle mass.

I have noticed a marked increase in my energy levels. My balance still sucks.

I had blood work done recently, the following are my results:

Reverse t3 - 20 (not sure what it says for my leptin levels)

LDL - 116

Total cholesterol - 220

HDL - 85

BUN/Creatin ratio went up to 27(my doctor says I was dehydrated)

Plan to have more labs as I can afford them

would be better,
but
for starters you can get:
Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum
that is why I mentioned that in my previous post.
That test will tell also about your need for seafood.

\------------------------------------------
About T3 & Rt3
they are identical chemically but one is right the other left.
RT3 protect you from excess of FreeT3
Both attach to receptors.
There is limited ## number of receptors.
The more RT3 there is the less is left for T3.
You want make all FreeT3 busy.
Eventually the best you can do is to have
FreeT3 within top 1/8 of laboratory range or even slightly over.
RT3 at mid range or lower.
Others posted versions see bottom of this post:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/#post-89859](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/#post-89859)
but
Having FreeT3 and RT3 in some ideal place
does not guarantee you much
or
there is a guarantee that less than some ideal range may be just one of many problems.
So, you must have it in good range,
but you still must be vigilant.

### JanSz — 2017-06-14T17:14:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-216158>

> [brookway17 said:](/goto/post?id=216119)
>
> Thanks so much again for all your help Jan. You don't know how grateful I am. I talked to the people at Bodybio and they told me that they were unable to give out any information on the doctors they work with because I have a new FDA regulation. You know the FDA is always looking out for our best interests. Not. I like my integrative doctor but he is into a plant-based diet approach that I do not agree with.
> I spend the entire afternoon, outside at least, talking to LabCorp and Medicare trying to determine if I was going to be covered. And I could not get a straight answer out of them. Do I need a particular code to get Medicare to cover the Fatty acid profile? I know the Medicare code (apt 82542) but I'm not sure if I need a code for my condition? I know that I have to fast for this test, are there any other test in that list that require fasting.
> Oh by the way, it's nice to meet someone older than myself on this forum.

I am covered by Medicare.
You are covered by Medicare.
The script below went thru in February 2017, without a problem.
The numbers at the bottom are the ICD-10 codes that I use.
They should be ok for you. (Assuming that your doc cooperates).
You can either copy/print the picture or copy/paste text below into excel spreadsheet.
Before you attempt to copy,
put my post in response mode, that will get you large part of formatting that you may need.
Then work out the rest of formatting looking at the picture.
When you print leave very small margin on top
so in doctor's office they can xerox in the prescription header
so the final product looks like in my first picture.
At the LabCorp be 30-45 min before they open.
Mine open at 7:00AM
Be fasted for 12 hrs
Drink 12-16oz water at 6AM
For a week do not eat any fats that you usually do not eat.
This script calls for second blood draw at noon.
That is for cortisol, also it is opportunity for phlebotomist to get your blood again if he/she had any mishap processing your morning blood.
\================================================

Basically my immune system is attacking my lower brainstem which is responsible for all control of motor functions in the body. Imuran suppresses the production of T cells in my bone marrow.
> I was hoping that CT could lower my information and possibly get me off of the steroids.

[https://rarediseases.info.nih.gov/d...rivascular-enhancement-responsive-to-steroids](https://rarediseases.info.nih.gov/diseases/10779/chronic-lymphocytic-inflammation-with-pontine-perivascular-enhancement-responsive-to-steroids)

**Summary**

**Chronic lymphocytic inflammation with pontine perivascular enhancement responsive to steroids (CLIPPERS)** is an inflammatory disease of the central nervous system. The main symptoms of CLIPPERS include double vision, nystagmus, uncoordinated movement (ataxia) and facial numbness or tingling. This condition can be treated by suppressing the immune system with [steroids](http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/steroids.html).
Last updated: 5/4/2012
**Treatment**

The signs and symptoms of CLIPPERS typically improve after treatment with [steroids](http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/steroids.html). Initial treatment may involve a short course of high dose steroids given intravenously, and then oral steroids. Many patients experience a relapse when steroids are tapered off, so it is usually necessary to continue treatment that suppresses the immune system. Long term treatment may include a low dose of oral steroids and another type of immune suppressant, such as [methotrexate](http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/druginfo/meds/a682019.html) or [rituximab](http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/druginfo/meds/a607038.html). Because there have been very few patients with CLIPPERS reported in medical journals, the best course of treatment has not yet been determined.[\[1\]](https://rarediseases.info.nih.gov/diseases/10779/chronic-lymphocytic-inflammation-with-pontine-perivascular-enhancement-responsive-to-steroids#ref_7343)
Last updated: 4/11/2014
/////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
/////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
**Steroids**
Also called: Corticosteroids, Glucocorticoids
\===========================================
[http://www.rxlist.com/imuran-drug.htm](http://www.rxlist.com/imuran-drug.htm)
**IMURAN**
(azathioprine) 50-mg Scored Tablets
**WARNING
MALIGNANCY**
Chronic immunosuppression with IMURAN, a purine antimetabolite increases risk of malignancy in humans.
\=============================================
[https://medlineplus.gov/druginfo/meds/a682019.html](https://medlineplus.gov/druginfo/meds/a682019.html)
Methotrexate may cause very serious, life-threatening side effects. You should only take methotrexate to treat cancer or certain other conditions that are very severe and that cannot be treated with other medications.
\=============================================
[https://medlineplus.gov/druginfo/meds/a607038.html](https://medlineplus.gov/druginfo/meds/a607038.html)
Rituximab Injection

\==============================================

Natural Total Testosterone should be about 600-700ng/dl(348-1197)

Ask your doc to give you script for
Androgel 1.62%
4 pumps/day

two months latter get tested
the day of testing take your Androgel 1.62% AFTER blood was drawn.
Adjust your dosing so you would newer get more than about 1500ng/dl on tests.

DHEAs
Estradiol

//
Doctor may insist on testing month after starting Androgel.
Well, you would have to oblige if he asks, but that will extend your adjustment period.
Reason for 2 months:
Using external testosterone will suppress your LH & FSH and therefore testicular production.
This process takes time, you body needs to adjust to that.

Additionally, unless you also use HCG injections, you will loose most or all of your scrotum and testicles.
To save on appearances, HCG injections are used. Using HCG is optional and is often hard to get script from doctor for HCG.
That can be discussed further.

I am not sure of how much I understand of what Jack was attempting to tell me.
but:
optimal mitochondria have certain size
less than optimal mitochondria expands and makes less EZ
That is a one problem.
The other problem is in cytosol.

I think instead of being made of EZ only or mostly
the amount of bulk water increases (in less than optimal condition)
\---------------------------------------------
The cytosol or cytoplasmic matrix is the liquid found inside cells. It constitutes most of the intracellular fluid (ICF).
The cytosol is a complex mixture of substances dissolved in water. Although water forms the large majority of the cytosol, its structure and properties within cells is not well understood.
[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytosol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytosol)

Basic daily dose used for people complaining of adrenal fatigue.
or
assume removed or non-functional adrenal glands
(can be increased by 2-3x in time of stress)

cortisone acetate 37 1/2 (weak, not recommended)
hydrocortisone 30 mg
cortef 30 mg
prednisone 7 1/2 mg
prednisolone 7 1/2 mg
triamcinolone 6 mg
methylprednisolone 6 mg
\*\*dexamethasone 1 mg
betamethasone 0.8 mg

DHEAs high
it is not about DHEAs or DHEA
measure your
estradiol (20-40 pg/mL) ---->LabCorp--->(estradiol,sensitive)
Estradiol, Sensitive Test Number: 140244 CPT Code: 82670
and
estrone (mid laboratory range)
if they are over it may be due to too much DHEAs or DHEA
\----------------------------------------------------------------------------
I am saying DHEAs
you are replying DHEA
which one was actually measured?

note
DHEAs = steady, measurements are reliable
DHEA = variable, measurements are not reliable

### drezy — 2017-06-27T17:31:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-217246>

Wow you're just moving along and outside now too. I'll try to hit the easier question first from my perspective.

> How cold do I need to get the water in the bath?

I worked down to 54ish. My son nailed 58 on the first try. My wife needed to start at 64 and ease down 2 degrees at a time to 54ish. She still prefers 58-60.

I live 20000 gallons of Texas aboveground rainwater tanks. My water's temperature is ridiculous because of that, which is why I engineered my Franken-cooler. At 83F water temp it take 30-40 lbs to cool my water down to 55ish.

Use this calculator for a rough guide. [http://www.onlineconversion.com/mixing\_water.htm](http://www.onlineconversion.com/mixing_water.htm).

Assuming your tub contains ~35gal ( 132489 grams ) and your water temp is 70F. @20 Lbs ice should get you near 56 ish. Stirring will speed the cooling. Room/air temp doesn't factor in quite as much for initial cooling.

You're going to unrinate like a racehorse before the derby. That's good. Don't stop the water.

For the other questions you kind of have to engage the doc in your own head and remain attentive to your own body. There's no penalty for going slow, although jumping in feet first is thrilling.

> **\***Cold Tub step!Full Body Immersion with Ice:
>
> -   Fill the bathtub with cold tap water
> -   Wear socks, gloves, and a knitted cap to keep the heat in your body
> -   Add 20 lbs of ice to chest and abdomen
> -   When your body is use to the ice you can remove the socks, gloves, and cap
> -   **DO NOT** let your skin temperature fall below 50-55 degrees
> -   Stay for 45 minutes
> -   Continue daily 2-5 times a week
>
> Full Body Immersion with NO Ice:
>
>
> -   Jump into a pool, lake, or hot tub from the neck down
> -   Stay for 10-20 minutes
> -   Pay attention to skin color

Oddly, nowadays if I do the smallest workout my muscle mass increases fairly quickly while also quickly shedding body fat. I didn't see that coming. Seems like the body has a plan, but I'm not particularly a fan of much muscle so I keeping workouts few and far between (and outside of course).

Nice that keeps it simple.

I went straight into the ice water. I found Jack when I was trying to explain why the heck I was losing weight just doing mid-day walks. The calorie burn physics weren't explaining it to me. I read about the ice water and thought it was pretty crazy and was way outside my model of how life works. I wanted to either disprove him or expand my model. I stopped all supplements and workouts (which were few and far between anyway). Model expanded!

After 3 sessions per week for 2 weeks it became easy and just a habit. The same is true for my wife. The cold (and morning light) improves her sleep(always been her issue) and stress response. I can't recall a single time in the past when she had 2 consecutive good nights of sleep. Now whole weeks of good sleep are her norm. Major improvement to the relationship right there.

In fact If I were a marriage counselor I'd have a lever that flipped both partners through a trap door into ice water for their 45 minute session with me. Cryotherapy meets therapist!

"Let's see so you're both putting on weight. She's not respecting you as a man and he's not listening to you and keeps googling the neighbor? I've got just the thing <SHUNK>."

Maybe the return visits would be the tricky part of that business model.

I managed to hold it for 45 minutes checking my skin temperature every 15, I got my skin temperature below 50°F. Tonight I will use the ice that was left over from last night to call the tub down before I add 40 pounds of new ice.
I have been wondering what the trade off of adaption is between the ice on the torso and the simple immersion. When I hold the bag of ice on my torso I get my skin temperature down on my torso to less then 50°F , although a smaller surface area . But when I immerse myself in 55°F water my skin temperature is above 55°F. But the area is greater

### JanSz — 2017-07-27T14:05:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-220195>

> [brookway17 said:](/goto/post?id=220148)
>
> [View attachment 5419](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/5419/) Jan, my latest bloodwork in full:
>
> [View attachment 5419](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/5419/)

Pete;
\============================
Thyroid works with proper cortisol.
Proper=level and time, Circadian rhythm
It is good idea to have a snap in time of
cortisol and thyroid panel.

\============================

T3 and RT3 are identical chemically, but one is right the other left.
T3=active
RT3=passive
They fit into the same receptors.
Amount of receptors is limited.
When receptor is occupied with RT3 it is disabled,
Too much RT3 prevents FT3 from doing its job (even if you would have more FT3 which you do not)

You are missing on FT3
You have excessive RT3

Assuming all else works as it should, you nee to get rid of some RT3 and get more FT3.

Good goal is to have
FT3 in upper 1/4 of laboratory range or on top of range or even slightly over.
RT3 would be nice to have in mid range.

There are also equations for fine tuning (but not sure of ones ability to make those desired changes).

Selenium, Lugols=(iodine+potassium iodide) come first.

Spectracell Micronutrient Analysis would be helpfull here.

\--------------
To get your FT3 & RT3 in line
you may want to try
Cytomel=T3 (or other T3)
T3 is better to take 2-3 times a day
for testing purpose, figure out convenient schedule (but use most of your daily dose more toward early part of the day).
On the day of blood draw for testing, take your T3 (after) blood draw.

Eventually you may get into position where you should use a combination of
T3 and NTH (natural thyroid hormone).

There are conversion tables, they look good on paper.
They do not always work as expected but I do not have anything better.

\=====================
You may get some useful information on this post:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/low-cortisol-levels.14194/page-16#post-178931](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/low-cortisol-levels.14194/page-16#post-178931)

### brookway17 — 2017-07-27T15:26:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-220202>

Jan, thanks for all your informative input on my thyroid issues. I went to stop the thyroid madness and calculated my T3 /rt3 ratio = 6.1
My doctor has prescribed me a slow release t3 from a compounding pharmacy. Do you think this is better than Cytomel?

### JanSz — 2017-07-27T15:51:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-220204>

> [brookway17 said:](/goto/post?id=220202)
>
> Jan, thanks for all your informative input on my thyroid issues. I went to stop the thyroid madness and calculated my T3 /rt3 ratio = 6.1
> My doctor has prescribed me a slow release t3 from a compounding pharmacy. Do you think this is better than Cytomel?
> I have read some comments in the forums that some people experienced problems dealing with the cold immersion when on t3. I kind of thought If my t3 was the low, I would have a problem turning on my pilot light.

You have to figure your own experience.
Slow release vs "regular" T3 (that you may or may not have to take 2-3x a day.
You may also measure your temperatures (under tongue) to see if it corresponds with T3 intake.

I have frequently heard of people complaining that some thyroid hormone products do not work as expected.
Usually this type of problems are easily solved when chewing those tablets first.
I always chew those pills.
If you are inclined that way, then using slow release product may not be the best.
If you chew it, you would likely defeat the slow release process.
If you do not chew it and current script re-fill works, that may not be the case with next re-fill (by definition, compounding stuff is made once at the time, over time they change (for hopefully better components or fillers, that not always works as expected).

My biggest difficulties (with testing) is to be on some kind of rigid and logical schedule so you can make better sense titrating the good dose for you.

Indeed, your good CT resistance and experience somehow does not correlate with expected thyroid levels.
But it indicate that you are in good shape and may tolerate (even) one T3 daily dose all at once.

\====

### JanSz — 2017-07-28T18:16:58+0300

> [brookway17 said:](/goto/post?id=220309)

> Jan, by chewing do you mean sub lingual absorption as opposed to swolling?

No.
Chewing and then swallowing, assures absorption.
It is not about sublingual absorption.
It is about assuring that good absorption would take place.
Some products, specially more known like Armour thyroid, had a changed composition.
Previously one of the best known, currently doubtful.
So,
there is a possibility that the problem is not with the product, but with its absorption, and possibly can be by-passed by chewing.
\------

If you are using T3,
if you feel good on T3
your low FreeT3 on blood test may possibly be explained by long time between last T3 dose and blood draw.
But,
I would expect your RT3 to be lower and it is not.

### drezy — 2017-07-31T04:45:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-220486>

Bad news. We all met behind your back and decided that you need to be frozen into a large block of ice every day of the week. Some members disagreed but eventually came around to voting that we should leave your face protruding out of the block for oxygenation.

Seriously though there are manifold (<--- only because it sounds smarter than multiple) reasons that that is a very complicated question. You know that I support your journey so let me take a couple liberties after I explain my own CT position.

My own mother is 20 years older than I am. Without getting into details she's been falling apart since 40 or 45 (teeth, blood sugar, and vein blockages). I'm 40. I want nothing to do with any of this business, so my ass is in 52 degree water 3-5 times per week. To be honest I've been experimenting with lower temperatures vs double the 52 degree sessions(one AM one PM) per week.

How long do I have to do it thanks to my haplotype? I plan on doing it until I die. I think as I age I may increase the amount that I do CT. I'm in it to win it as much a I can.

Do you know why I sent you the meter? Have you ever looked up astronaut viral shedding?

### JanSz — 2017-08-05T16:51:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-220973>

Peter
Wonder if I could interest you in DHA story, possibly from different angle.
Something I was not able to achieve to date no matter how hard I tried.
We have one side from Jack.
Nobody argues that DHA is important.
That is established fact and by now there is little need in reinforcing that part.

On the other hand, majority of neolitic diseases presents with high DHA.
DHA is volatile (one treats hand granate gently and with respect)

I think that really means that no matter how good is
(desirable DHA level)
we should be real careful with excesses.
It is very hard to get any traction in this line of logic.
Not only DHA also EPA.
When one looks at them in most research papers, usually the two, (DHA & EPA) are lumped together, likely on the purpose so it would be difficult to parse between the two.
Research are made in such a way that if someone benefited (from the desirable EPA level), sky is moved, trumpets are playing, that is taken as a proof that excessive amounts of EPA are even better.

..
Possibly think about that and share any thoughts that you may have.

..
Recently, I am even starting my doubts when speaking about the correct meaning of 4:1 ratio.
My understanding of what 4:1 ratio means is that
we should eat food that have four (4) parts of LA for a one part of ALA.
That is what Yehuda said.

In my recent personal adjustments I am coming somewhat short of ALA on my blood tests.
But that may be just my personal n=1.

That only reinforces my still standing conclusion that there is practically no way to judge fatty acids status without testing.

....
...

### drezy — 2017-08-14T17:34:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-221650>

The easiest framework to set up in your head is to ask:
"In the environment I'm in would one naturally be eating this?"

Your tub, though artificial, = sitting in cold water/winter signaling
So

In cold water/winter were humans enjoying coconuts? No

Jack's said that in winter(around sept/oct) he switches to lard/animal fat/duck fat

In cold water/winter were humans enjoying fattened animals? Sure

It's not a "my paleo is bigger than your paleo" contest either. The food you eat is information.

### Dennis Clark — 2017-08-26T18:26:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-222305>

Hi, Pete: We have advantages/disadvantages for adopting parts of the Epi-Paleo lifestyle here in Phoenix. The big advantage, of course, is sunshine. Every day (mostly) I can go out for early morning sun, then again around mid-day to get a good dose of UVB, then again in the late afternoon. Long ago I adapted to the summer heat (e.g., endurance running at noon in July - which seems silly now). Even just a small dabbling into CT a couple of years ago further built up my heat tolerance (as well as my cold tolerance). The main disadvantage is outdoor CTing. I don't yet have a good, cost-effective way to make my 100-gallon cattle trough cold when the air temp is 90-110 F. (I did finally locate a local gym that accepts my Silver Sneakers card and has a cold plunge; alas the 'cold' was only 76 F - fully 20 degrees too warm!) On a personal note, my new home is in an area with no aboveground power lines; the house has no dirty electricity in any outlet (my previous home was 10 times acceptable levels or more); a nearby green belt is handy for doing sprint intervals barefoot.

By the way, since 'discovering' this lifestyle I have incorporated lots of poke (PO-kay) into my diet - you can Google poke recipes and see what I mean. Raw oily fish is my ace-in-the-hole source of DHA now.

Just some thoughts for the greater good.

Cheers,
Dennis

### brookway17 — 2017-08-26T23:48:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-222313>

Hey Dennis, nice to hear from you again. We only get UVB about six months of the year, which I try my hardest to get as much as I can. The bigger problem has been gaining morning son, it's been overcast a lot in the morning here lately. The last several mornings I have luked out, and my sleep has been better. I consider us believe to be a key ingredient for me to repair all the damage done by the auto immune condition. I have been hoping that getting cold adapted will improve my sleep, amongst other things.

> Even just a small dabbling into CT a couple of years ago

Are you still cold adapted ? How long did it take for you to get cold adapted ?
I too am lucky and have underground powerlines in my development. How did you measure the dirty electricity? When I moved here 25 years ago we had artesian well water, no fluoride. But as the area grew they got water from Philadelphia which was fluorinated. Now I use RO water for my cold bath
Poke sounds interesting, I found a restaurant in Philadelphia, Hawaiian, that offers this. My kids live down in Philadelphia so I will have to ask them if they know about it. However I have to be careful about eating raw seafood because I am immuno suppressed.

### Dennis Clark — 2017-08-27T18:31:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-222364>

Hi, Pete...Yes, I am still cold adapted - a least a little. When I had a pool and the water temp got down to 55 F, it took me about 2 months, 3 times a week for 30-60 minutes ea., to really feel a difference. Now I do some face dunking, and I have a couple of ice vests from [https://coolfatburner.com/](https://coolfatburner.com/) to fill in the gaps. The sunshine you get through clouds can have significant amounts of UVA and IRA, even if no UVB. Regarding the dirty electricity, I came across Graham Stetzer meter and filters (see: [http://www.dirtyelectricity.ca/](http://www.dirtyelectricity.ca/)) - although I bought mine through Amazon. My filters are in storage now, since I don't need them anymore. I got a lot out of a book by Lloyd Burrell about EMF hypersensitivity and how to measure and reduce nnEMFs (see: [https://www.electricsense.com/](https://www.electricsense.com/)). My Trifield meter (Trifield 100XE EMF Meter, also from Amazon) was indispensable in evaluating (and rejecting) potential new homes. About DHA: do whatever you can to jack up your DHA (sn-2 form from actual fish). The wildest strategy that I've seen on the forum (and used once myself) is Inger's mackerel head smoothie (I wrote about it here \[with her video link\]: [https://herbscientist.com/mackerel-head-smoothies-an-epicurean-delight/](https://herbscientist.com/mackerel-head-smoothies-an-epicurean-delight/)). My latest experiment was to get a large salmon head (99 cents a pound at a local Chinese market) and pluck out and eat a raw eye. Disgusting! Great source of DHA, though. I don't know about your headache, although the first thing that comes to mind is the dehydrating effects of CT. Could be a link there.

Sharing the road to Optimal,
Dennis

### brookway17 — 2017-08-27T23:35:23+0300

Hey Dennis, thanks for responding. I have the same meter. Recently Drezy lent me his digital corenet electro smog meter . It caught some stuff right my meter had missed.
I did a fatty acids profile and it showed I had high DHA and EPA. I eat raw oysters. I also like sockeye salmon, which is supposed to be very high in DHA.
Thanks for the links on dirty electricity.
I belong to a gym also, unfortunately it is lite with toxic florescents, like most gyms are unless they have upgraded to the even more toxic LEDs. I like the yoga classes, it has help me with my balance issues. I also need to use machines for strength training because I would fall over with free weights. Now I tend to do yoga on my patio.

### Dennis Clark — 2017-08-31T21:26:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-222670>

Gotta love the humor here, drezy. I hope the image I uploaded shows up. Oh, and if you want your pheromones to be more effective, you might have to stop bathing.

Pete: the weightlifting strategy I first heard of is the Nautilus method, invented by [Arthur Jones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arthur_Jones_\(inventor\)); in his book, he emphasized continuous rotational resistance, which he created the Nautilus machines for. Since it is a brand name, it also goes under the name "Super Slo-Mo." I discovered the best and most current explanation of the physiology behind it in a book by Doug McGuff and John Little (see [http://www.drmcguff.com/](http://www.drmcguff.com/)). They added the "Time Under Load" concept to this approach. They also posted videos on youtube, which I linked to here: [https://herbscientist.com/fitness/](https://herbscientist.com/fitness/). (That page links to my fitness site, which I have subsequently suspended.) McGuff also markets [his services](http://www.drmcguff.com/), which are a little pricey ... not to mention that he is located in North Carolina, I think. We all now, on this forum, refer to it as the high-intensity method. All the same horses of the same color, as far as I can tell. One of the real important keys is to not overdo it ... 7 days between workouts, minimum. I like that. It appeals to my inner lazybones.

As for my workout environment, it is too much to expect incandescent lighting in the age of fluorescents, CFLs, LEDs, and the like. Bummer. I just minimize my exposure (once a week, max). All the big gyms are lighting disasters. When I am by myself in my local "senior" rec center, I can turn the lights off completely. Yay! At some point I might seriously consider adopting something along the lines of [movnat](https://www.movnat.com/). I am too cheap to buy coaching from those folks, though. I just do my own research and bio-hacking.

Oh, I think you asked once about how to find a doctor who would order the lab tests you want. Was it you? I skip the doctor completely and order my own tests through the [Life Extension Organization](http://www.lifeextension.com/). Dr. K might have an affiliate link to them somewhere. If so, you can help him out by ordering through his link (I don't know where that is, though). LEF has an annual sale on lab tests in May/June, I think. Their prices are super low, and they send the results directly to you.

^^^ Lissajous - It won't get you any dates but it sure looks cool and might help your scrabble game (even if it's solitaire scrabble).

Some people including caroline have said that colder is easier.

I've been using my incredible powers of cognitive dissonance to convince myself that I'm not too lazy to make adjustments to my tub thermostat as the season cools, but instead purposely letting my tub get colder in a brilliant experiment because the information would be useful to Pete (see I can do that with cognitive dissonance --it's like the quantum strangeness of mental functions). I can say that the 45-48 F water seems easier to get into like it's insta-numbing my surface.

About 2 weeks into r 3x regularly weekly it just became easier. It went from "damn cold" to "invigorating cold". I feel a little rambunctious too and tell my wife that if she loves me she has to hug me to prove it. It ends in a hug either way. I wonder if this explains some of the slightly rambunctious behavior of Vikings in the past... Maybe they were just misunderstood after all.

What particular difficulty or discomfort do you experience? How long do you stay cold after emerging from your glacial abyss?

### Dennis Clark — 2017-09-15T23:19:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-223761>

> [brookway17 said:](/goto/post?id=222793)
>
> Hey Dennis, I wanted to ask you some questions about your cold adaption, since you're an old guy like me.
> Did you notice that you were able to increase strength and muscle mass after you cold adapted?
> Did your testosterone levels increase?
> Also, what do you do to maintain your cold adaption?

Hi, Pete...Had a little hiccup lately...food poisoning and recovery from it knocked me out for nearly a week. Lost a bunch of weight (i.e., super dehydrated). Finally got my appetite back and got in a workout today. About your questions on CT: I haven't noticed any change in muscle mass or strength due to CT; other variables come into play, too (sleep, food, exercise). Haven't had my T level measured in a couple of years, since before I started CTing. (serum T was at 554 ng/dL in 2015).

My cold adaptation maintenance centers around the cold vests from [https://coolfatburner.com/](https://coolfatburner.com/). I started doing face dunking, too, just not consistently enough. I haven't yet set up a way to get my outdoor watering trough cold enough here in the Arizona summer. Come the first of October I will be ramping up that effort AND diving into ketosis for the winter. ('Winter' being a relative thing here...nothing like you have in Pennsylvania.)

The main change that I have observed from CTing is cold tolerance. For example, on a trip in March 2016 I wore shorts and short sleeves in winter weather in Northern California and Oregon; including a night on the way home in an RV park in Flagstaff, AZ, that got below freezing.

^^^^add sunlight to the mix. SUNLIGHT is tyrosine kinase inhibitor and any one with HC elevated lack sun.

HC is associated with heart disease, low NO, low K2 and low methylation and poor quantum yield. Basically Pete youre telling us you live in an environmental shit hole.

Electric fields control embryonic development and morphogenesis and wound healing through directing and enhancing cell migration and proliferation. They also control who gets heart disease and atherosclerosis. However, details pertaining to electric sensing of cells remain unclear to the paradigm if you read their literature. It is pretty clear cut in the bio-physical data. We need to eat saturated fat to maintain our coulomb electrostatic force in the mitochondrial matrix to maintain our redox potential. Being in the sun makes this easy. Many studies have reported involvement of different cell surface proteins, yet the identity of the electric field sensor is unknown to the paradigm. For my mitochondriac misfits it is linked to cholesterol, sphingolipids, DHA, and all the lipid rafts in our arteries. This is true in even in those with hypercholesterolemia. High cholesterol means nothing if there is no calcium in your arteries. properly charge ipid rafts in arteies make you resistant to heart disease. They act as the primary sensor to electric field-induced directional cell migration for morphogenesis, pathologic plaque formation, and sustained blood flow as we age. These nanodomains in lipid rafts respond to electric fields from sunlight as mobile complexes that polarize, coalesce, and partition membrane proteins and in turn activate intracellular signaling events to orient cell migration and keep our vessels patent and responsive to the nitric oxide effects of sunlight.

K2 is a vitamin that is a lipid and part of the lipid raft in arteries. Lipid rafts are electric field sensors in vessels. Nobody realizes that yet but my misfits here in my tribe but I am OK with that. K2 and HC are markers of the lipid raft. They are there to sense the electric fields of sunlight after they crash into RBC's in the blood plasma. How does the blood plasma get closer to the skin surface? Sunlight stimulates the release of nitric oxide to bring the vessels closer to the skin for blood to be irradiated by sunlight. That is how it works. K2 is a vitamin made by the gut microbiome using light release from the prokaryotes there. Bacteria (prokaryotes) are well known to release 5000 times more light than our cells. Since K2 is made from aromatic amino acids these aromatic rings of this chemical made by symbionts in out gut capture the light released and create Vitamin K2. This K2 is then ferried by more lipid carriers in blood that also are electric field sensors for sunlight for deposition in your arterial wall that wait for the sunlight stimulus to do their part in keeping you healthy in full spectrum sunlight. Tracking your plaque index is possible these days using Xray. I am not a fan of this test chronically but it is not a bad idea to do when you decide to initially become a mitochondriac one time to get a baseline assessment. There is another indirect assessment of vitamin K2 activity that gets straight to the heart of the calcium paradox that is tumping the lipidologists today. Coronary artery calcium scoring is a specialized type of ultra-fast X-ray (not fast enough for me) imaging that measures the presence and amount of calcium buildup in the arteries that supply blood and oxygen to your heart. If your main goal of K2 replacement with foods or supplementation is reducing your risk of heart attack, this might be an important test to take, to measure that risk. Coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring, also called calcium score or heart scan, is a technique that uses computed tomography (CT) scanning technology to quantify the volume and density of calcium in each of your coronary arteries. The calcium presence is calculated to give you a “score” or number that represents your arterial calcium burden in your arteries and this effects the amount of NO present to vasodialate our circulation in a mitochondrial stresses environment.

Tom Petty could have used this. Remember that arterial calcification is an active process mediated by bone-building cells present in bone marrow and this is connected to the blood plasma system to make connections globally in the body over hydrogen bonding networks in blood. (93% water by volume). A lack of K2 causes calcium to accumulate, and NO to decline, within plaque in a consistent ratio, occupying about 20 percent of plaque volume. For that reason, the amount of arterial calcium detected with a heart scan reflects the buildup of atherosclerotic plaque and the higher stimulus from sunlight required to get the same effect if the local mitochondria heteroplasmy levels were LOWER. In cardiac disease they are always elevated. This test cannot directly tell us that, but it indirectly implies it to the mitchondriac.. Vitamin K2 deficiency isn't the only factor contributing to heart disease, but undercarboxylated MGP (matrix gla protein) levels, a sure sign of K2 deficiency, do correlate to the severity of arterial calcification and the CAC score. I wrote about this in my K2 blogs on my site. The greater the degree of K2 deficiency, the greater your NO decline in your vessels, and the higher the calcium score will be. A high CAC score on electron beam computed tomography— is a precise type of CT, discussed in this book below. It turns out to be a way better predictor of mortality than is age is because it is an indirect measure of heteroplasmy rates in your coronary bed and heart. We are only as strong as our weakest link in quantum biology. That means you are as old as your arteries and this test will tell you If you need more sunlight than your current environment could provide. This could lead you to migrating to abetter location to get healthier. For example, if you are a 60-year-old man with a low CAC score, there's a good chance you'll live to a ripe old age. On the flipside, if you are a 45-year-old man with a heavy calcium plaque burden, low NO levels, and higher heteroplasmy rates your more likely to become Tom Petty. Heart disease is major killer of humans today because it is a circadian disease too! Most people die at dawn. Your are more likely to be one of the unfortunate souls who suffer a massive heart attack at age 50— if you don't take action to prevent it by lowering your blue light life and nnEMF burdens. Sudden death from heart attack is much more highly correlated with arterial calcification than with altered cholesterol panels based upon the latest in the literature. You might not hear that in the office because the data is relatively new to most clinicians. The biggest advantage of CAC scoring is that it quantifies your risk of heart attack and is a decent indirect measure of heteroplasmy rate in the heart. It is a hack I did a long time ago and I thought I'd mention it here. A low score means low risk, a high score means a high risk. No other risk factor offers that kind of graded risk assessment.

### drezy — 2017-10-11T20:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-225597>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=225553)
>
> If you try going deeper into methylation rabbit hole, in my opinion it is kind of fruitless/hopeless.

Thumbs up on that one. I spent a few months over-obsessing on the topic.

Disclaimer: @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) I'll give it a go. I'm not the possessor of the full objective truth about MTHFR so everything I write below is only from my n=1 experience. I'll drop footnotes below this in case they are useful just ignore them if they are overly-detailed or overly subjective. My goal is to die more slowly and healthy and these are my notes.

1.  Understand the problem --> Amongst a family of other related issues (\*1) MTHFR will reduce your efficiency in creating an enzyme that converts homocysteine into methionine. To keep it simple too much homocysteine & not enough methionine. (you can search those two terms for all the related issues that come along with them my unaddressed symptom is high blood pressure). There are 2 SNPs that MTHFR is encoded in making for varying levels of reduced enzyme activity. (\*2)
2.  Common solutions with no showing off -->
    1.  If it comes in a box, if it's canned, has a marketing campaign or a logo, is served at a restaurant, is made with flour, has a clown as a mascot, is processed, or is "fortified" in any way the DO NOT EAT IT. It will be fortified with folic acid and your folate processing already sucks. (\*3).
    2.  I rarely ever write about supplementation, but I found this helpful initially and reserve a stash in case I have to endure a crap environment temporarily. Price shop a bit: [https://www.amazon.com/Neuro-Immune...30546&sr=8-2&keywords=neuro+immune+stabilizer](https://www.amazon.com/Neuro-Immune-Stabilizer-Topical-Cream-Ounces/dp/B00DDIRNYC/ref=sr_1_2_a_it?ie=UTF8&qid=1507730546&sr=8-2&keywords=neuro+immune+stabilizer)
    3.  Detox - Do not dive head first into detox. This is covered here regularly but is still worth a big mention. Even if you try the above product ease it in nice and slow and be on alert for any changes.(\*4)
3.  This may end up apply to Pete so I'm posting it here. I can repost over there if you'd like.
4.  Credit where it's due, The Epi-Paleo diet and LWM from this site helps tremendously. I actively push for 3 days of cold sessions immediately followed by solar noon sun per week. That's built into my life right now.
5.  Have a healthy dose of skepticism about all of my claims. If you have MTHFR you likely have have homocysteine. If you have high homocysteine you likely have high blood pressure. It's really easy to establish a baseline and measure BP as you try to optimize. Don't overlook your symptom as your quick and easy test.(\*5) Also please keep in mind this is genetic meaning that it may or may not be expressed I think depending largely on the environment you,your mitochondrial colonies, and your bacterial gut colonies are subjected to.

It's a topic that is important to me and I tried above to keep it as concise and basic as I could. I agree very much with @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) 's sentiment that it can really get over hyped for various reasons.

If you have any questions corrections or thoughts please let me know. I wrote it up in between some other activity and tried to stay coherent. I'll clarify or correct it if it would help make it more generally useful.

Footnotes
\------------------
\*1 - Potentially compromised myelination, DNA & RNA production, glutathione...
\*2 - Rough breakdown of enzyme functionality/risk in the "Genomics and Cardiovascular Risk" Graphic here:
\*3- Canned/jared oysters and fish are up to your discretion though fresher is better. Folic acid is a synthetic folate. Natural greens have methylfolate that is more bio-available/active than the synthetic form (no surprises there). Synthetic folic acid can "gum up" the works and I think can potentially crowd out methylfolate in MTHFR people. Much of what is conventionally considered "detox" at health food stores can actually backfire on you including certain vegetables. My grocer checkout dude is surprised at how few fruits and vegetables I purchase.
\*4 - I never had a problem with the topical cream but some people experience heightened anxiety when/if they slam 5-MTHF. So go easy.
\*5 - It sounds a little nutty, but to get a quick gauge of my blood pressure(when not near my BP meter) I touch my middle finger as far down toward my wrist as possible. If it feels cold I know I likely did something, ate something, or positioned myself(environmentally) incorrectly. Using this to understand crap environments was a really big win for me.

### JanSz — 2017-10-18T22:11:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-226171>

> [brookway17 said:](/goto/post?id=226162)
>
> I was having trouble with my T3. My TSH and T4 are in the normal range but my liver is **not converting T4 to T3**. Is there anything in particular I should be doing to help my liver?

sonic hedgehog
[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sonic\_hedgehog](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sonic_hedgehog)

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deiodinase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deiodinase)
[Iodotyrosine deiodinase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iodotyrosine_deiodinase) contributes to breakdown of thyroid hormones. It releases [iodine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iodine), for renewed use, from iodinated tyrosines resulting from catabolism of iodothyronines. Iodotyrosine deiodinase employs a flavin mononucleotide cofactor and belongs to the NADH oxidase/flavin reductase superfamily.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deiodinase#cite_note-iodityrDeio-1)

[Iodothyronine deiodinases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iodothyronine_deiodinase) catalyze release of iodine directly from the thyronine hormones. They are selenocysteine-dependent membrane proteins with a catalytic domain resembling [Peroxiredoxins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peroxiredoxin) (Prx).[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deiodinase#cite_note-ThyronineDio-2) Three related isoforms, deiodinase type I, II, and III, contribute to activation and inactivation of the initially released hormone precursor T4 ([thyroxine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thyroxine)) into T3 ([triiodothyronine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triiodothyronine)) or rT3 ([reverse triiodothyronine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reverse_triiodothyronine)) in target cells. The enzymes catalyze a reductive elimination of iodine (the different isoforms attack different thyronine positions), thereby oxidizing themselves similar to Prx, followed by a reductive recycling of the enzyme.

cortisol (a-THF) (b-THF)
cortisone (THE)
free cortisol
selenium
iodine
potassium iodide

if that does not help, eat T3

Get DUTCH, see your profiles

**anything else that works is always welcomed**

### JanSz — 2017-10-31T16:20:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-227026>

[http://deiodinase.org/teste/2013/05/14/deiodinase-2/](http://deiodinase.org/teste/2013/05/14/deiodinase-2/)

**Type 2 deiodinase (D2) is inactivated by ubiquitination.**
by [deiodinase](http://deiodinase.org/teste/author/deiodinase/) · May 14, 2013

The thyroid hormone activating deiodinase, D2, is a type I endoplasmic-reticulum (ER)-resident thioredoxin fold-containing selenoprotein. It is anchored to the ER membrane in close proximity to the cell nucleus through a single transmembrane domain. The D2 globular domain containing the catalytic active site is in the cytosol whereas only a few amino acids exist in the ER lumen. D2 is structured as a homodimer, D22, and monomers are inactive.

D2 has a short half-life that can be stabilized depending on whether its natural substrate T4 is available. In the presence of T4, D2 is inactivated with an approximately 20 min half-life whereas in the absence of T4 its half-life is prolonged to hours. This provides a mechanism through which the production of T3, the biologically active thyroid hormone, can be regulated according to the availability of T4. For example, an **accumulation of D2 in cells increases the fractional conversion of T4 to T3** when serum T4 levels are low, such as in the case of **iodine deficiency** or hypothyroidism. In contrast, serum levels of T4 are relatively high in patients maintained with levothyroxine replacement therapy, and the fractional conversion of T4 to T3 is consequently reduced.

D2 ubiquitination is the molecular mechanism underlying these changes in D2 half-life, i.e. the covalent attachment of multiple ubiquitin molecules to D2, which both inactivates the enzyme and targets it to degradation in the proteasomes. **Ubiquitination is thought to inactivate D2** by disrupting the conformation of the D22 dimer, critical for enzyme activity. A unique 18-amino acid loop confers intrinsic metabolic instability to D2, facilitating binding to proteins involved in the ubiquitination process.

The ubiquitin activating enzymes UBC6 and UBC7 are critical in the process of D2 ubiquitination, as well as two ubiquitin ligases, the hedgehog-inducible WSB-1, and TEB4, a ligase involved in the degradation of proteins in the endoplasmic reticulum. Ubiquitinated D2 (UbD2) is not immediately taken up by the proteasomes. Instead, UbD2 can be reactivated by de-ubiquitination, a process catalyzed by two USP-class D2-interacting de-ubiquitinases (DUBs), USP-20 and USP-33. The other two deiodinases, D1 and D3, are not known to be ubiquitinated or undergo post-translational modifications.

D2 ubiquitination occurs via K48-linked ubiquitin chains and exposure to its natural substrate, T4, accelerates UbD2 formation. UbD2 is taken up by proteasomes located in the cytoplasm. D2 retrotranslocation to the cytoplasm occurs via interaction with the p97-ATPase complex. D2 retrotranslocation also includes deubiquitination by the p97-associated DUB Ataxin-3. Once in the cytosol, D2 is delivery to the proteasomes as evidenced by coprecipitation with 19S proteasome subunit S5a and increased co-localization with the 20S proteasome.

D2 ubiquitination may occur at different rates depending on the cell where D2 is expressed. As opposed to the rest of the body, D2 in the hypothalamus is poorly ubiquitinated (or greatly de-ubiquitinated) sustaining conversion of T4 to T3 despite interaction with T4. As a result, TRH and TSH secretion are highly sensitive to serum T4. This also explains why serum TSH is normalized in many levothyroxine-treated patients even as serum T3 levels remain below the normal range.

\======================================
deiodinase 2 D2 DIO2 hypothyroid activity
\--------------------------------------

[https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13105-013-0307-y](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13105-013-0307-y)

**progesterone** withdrawal may underlie the decrement in D2 expression,
with consequent reduction in the peripheral conversion of T4 into T3
leading to a hypothyroid state.
\--------------------------------------
[http://deiodinase.org/2015/01/17/not-all-hypothyroid-patients-seem-to-be-created-equal/](http://deiodinase.org/2015/01/17/not-all-hypothyroid-patients-seem-to-be-created-equal/)

D2 gene polymorphism affects the brain: not all hypothyroid patients are the same. PMID: 25569702
a ‘polymorphism’, or a very prevalent mutation, in the D2 gene and found it to be **associated with insulin resistance**.

Note: insulin resistance=high insulin
\---------------------------------------

### JanSz — 2017-10-31T16:37:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-227027>

[http://deiodinase.org/teste/2016/01/13/the-history-and-future-of-treatment-of-hypothyroidism/](http://deiodinase.org/teste/2016/01/13/the-history-and-future-of-treatment-of-hypothyroidism/)

**The history and future of treatment of hypothyroidism.**
by [deiodinase](http://deiodinase.org/teste/author/deiodinase/) · January 13, 2016

Elizabeth A. McAninch, MD, and Antonio C. Bianco, MD, PhD **Annals of Internal Medicine. January 5, 2016**

**Abstract**
Thyroid hormone replacement has been used for more than a century to treat hypothyroidism. Natural thyroid preparations **(thyroid extract, desiccated thyroid, or thyroglobulin), which con- tain both thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3),** were the first pharmacologic treatments available and dominated the market for the better part of the 20th century. **Dosages were adjusted to resolve symptoms and to normalize the basal metabolic rate and/or serum protein-bound iodine level,** but thyrotoxic adverse effects were not uncommon. Two major developments in the 1970s led to a transition in clinical practice: 1) The development of the serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) radioimmunoas- say led to the discovery that many patients were overtreated, resulting in a dramatic reduction in thyroid hormone replace- ment dosage, and 2) the identification of peripheral deiodinase- mediated T4-to-T3 conversion provided a physiologic means to justify L-thyroxine monotherapy, obviating concerns about inconsistencies with desiccated thyroid. Thereafter, L-thyroxine mono- therapy at doses to normalize the serum TSH became the stan- dard of care. Since then, a subgroup of thyroid hormone–treated patients with residual symptoms of hypothyroidism **despite nor- malization of the serum TSH** has been identified. **This has brought into question the inability of L-thyroxine monotherapy to universally normalize serum T3 levels.** New research suggests mechanisms for the inadequacies of L-thyroxine monotherapy and highlights the possible role for personalized medicine based on **deiodinase polymorphisms**. Understanding the histor- ical events that affected clinical practice trends provides invalu- able insight into formulation of an approach to help all patients achieve clinical and biochemical euthyroidism.

\=============

**Is this only about deiodinase polymorphisms?**
**What are some other causes?**
How about bad progesterone levels or persistently high insulin?
...

### JanSz — 2017-10-31T18:34:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-227030>

[http://deiodinase.org/teste/2015/10...-iodothyronine-deiodinases-in-hypothyroidism/](http://deiodinase.org/teste/2015/10/09/scope-and-limitations-of-iodothyronine-deiodinases-in-hypothyroidism/)

**Scope and limitations of iodothyronine deiodinases in hypothyroidism.**
by [deiodinase](http://deiodinase.org/teste/author/deiodinase/) · October 9, 2015

Gereben B, McAninch EA, Ribeiro MO, Bianco AC. **Nature Review Endocrinology. 29 September 2015; doi:10.1038/nrendo.2015.155**

**Abstract**
The coordinated expression and activity of the iodothyronine deiodinases regulate thyroid hormone levels in hypothyroidism. Once heralded as the pathway underpinning adequate thyroid‐hormone replacement therapy with levothyroxine, the role of these enzymes has come into question as they have been implicated in both an inability to normalize serum levels of tri‐iodothyronine (T3) and the incomplete resolution of hypothyroid symptoms. These observations, some of which were validated in animal models of levothyroxine monotherapy, challenge the paradigm that tissue levels of T3 and thyroid‐hormone signalling can be fully restored by administration of levothyroxine alone. The low serum levels of T3 observed among patients receiving levothyroxine monotherapy occur as a consequence of type 2 iodothyronine deiodinase (DIO2) in the hypothalamus being fairly insensitive to ubiquitination. In addition, residual symptoms of hypothyroidism have been linked to a prevalent polymorphism in the DIO2 gene that might be a risk factor for neurodegenerative disease. Here, we discuss how these novel findings underscore the clinical importance of iodothyronine deiodinases in hypothyroidism and how an improved understanding of these enzymes might translate to therapeutic advances in the care of millions of patients with this condition.

[Download PDF](https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/78452284/2015-10%20-%20Nature%20Review%20Endocrinology.pdf)

### drezy — 2017-11-19T22:49:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-228436>

I'm out of my depths there.

I see Doc Hamblin advised the company so that's indicator #1 for me to get out the way.

More importantly, though, if you are experiencing and observing a difference and improvement then I think that's pretty powerful and speaks for itself.

For kicks I actually downloaded your video and tried to analyze it a little. One thing stood out. The lights seem to never fully dim (aka softer). Caveat:
The iPhone slo-mo is 240fps. The "flicker" itself was present on every other frame (aka the two zero crossings of 60Hz power).

### drezy — 2017-11-20T16:24:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-228489>

I think the benefits of pulsed light are still being discovered
From [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2933784/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2933784/)

> It was impossible to draw any meaningful correlations between pulse frequency and pathological condition, due to the wide-ranging and disparate data...

Having watched your video and the fact that it pulses every other frame @ 240fps. It looks like it's just a good old bridge rectifier that's inside every wall wart plug. Decrease that smoothing capacitor and voila you have an effective 120Hz pulse.

### JanSz — 2018-01-10T16:57:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-232176>

> [brookway17 said:](/goto/post?id=232165)
>
> Can I get my IGF one tested?

Yes you can.
It is a blood test.
But there is a problem with that testing.
There is at least two ways to do that testing.
Usually doctors do not specify how to do that test,
so laboratories do the cheap one, which is done automatically.
What you really want is the one that is done manually.

I had the opportunity to do mine both ways.
The results of automatic test are not even close to the real test.

> Hey Drezy, you had recommended Neuro-Immune Stabilizer Topical Cream it is comprised of 5-MTHF and vitamin B 12. Since I don’t have the MTHFR gene defect, do you still recommend it or should I just take sublingual vitamin B 12

To rephrase: Does it make sense for a person without MTHFR to take artificially methylated B?

I don't know. Farting around with any of the pathways without a really god understanding seems like asking for trouble.

FWIW I stopped using it completely for an experiment. I've been able to get at least 1.5 hours of UV4+ sun at least 3 times a week and my main symptom of crap methylation (BP) is easy to keep under control. Doc posted quite a bit about the NASA twin experiment and divergent methylation issues. That's seems to hold water in my case.

My big take away is that solar exposure and environment play a way bigger role than I even thought 6 months ago.

I also think being surrounded by natural sounds helps too. My new soundtrack is provided by mocking birds, house finches, and road runners that really don't like me being on their hunting ground. I'm also graced every morning by a vulture that stares down from his perch at me always at the ready to recycle my molecules.

### drezy — 2018-02-01T03:26:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-233889>

### drezy — 2018-02-22T15:53:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-235374>

> [brookway17 said:](/goto/post?id=235373)
>
> How do I know if I have gotten too much UVA?

I think for now too much UVA would be the least of your problems if one at all. I'd put it a couple notches below sock drawer organization.

Your meds may have some implications, but my own n=1 that make me not worry about too much anymore. In the dead of summer (July or August) I'd heard a while bunch from Doc and Wunsch abut this solar callous business. I decided to push it and spent all day (7AM Important - 7PM) outside on a day that hit UVindex 10+. I walked away just fine and looking only lightly more bronzed. I was pretty surprised by the result.

FYI: Your Philly UVindex these days would max out around 4 on the clearest of days. You've got to pass that by your n=1 and meds, but it's pretty easy sun.

### Sheddie — 2018-03-16T14:06:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-237295>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=237276)

Once upon a time a 1990s server with a metal fan was driving me nuts -- I could hear the whine. My IEEE-DH (now deceased) swapped the metal fan out for a 'plastic' fan. That could be a 'hack?' Wish I'd paid more attention back then and asked if harmonics would be reduced or mitigated... At least the audible annoyance went away... Would be interested in a current opinion if anyone has or will do that... especially metered results?

### brookway17 — 2018-03-16T18:11:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-237310>

Most electric motors, AC or DC, put out large magnetic fields and lots of dirty electricity. Transformers do a lot of bad things too but the worst offender switching power supplies like those found in CFL bulbs
I would switch off breakers except my house is wired in the way that all the bedrooms share different circuit breaker’s and if I shut off the breaker that controls the room I sleep in my wife would freak out if she didn’t have power. That is why I decided to go the filter route

### brookway17 — 2018-03-16T18:46:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-237318>

> Keep me informed on this. I have guesses but have never looked closely at a Joov. Fans can cause all kinds of local harmonics. Are the fans on all the time?

Yes, the fans on all the time. If I ever get around to designing a red light unit I think I would locate the Transformers and fans remotely. You could use a Centrifugal fan connected to the unit with a flexible duct and suck air through to cool the LEDs.

### drezy — 2018-03-17T01:21:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-237336>

> [Sheddie said:](/goto/post?id=237295)
>
> Once upon a time a 1990s server with a metal fan was driving me nuts -- I could hear the whine. My IEEE-DH (now deceased) swapped the metal fan out for a 'plastic' fan. That could be a 'hack?' Wish I'd paid more attention back then and asked if harmonics would be reduced or mitigated... At least the audible annoyance went away... Would be interested in a current opinion if anyone has or will do that... especially metered results?

That was an audio harmonic that you may have heard. It may have been transduced electrically, mechanically (wind), or a combination of both.

We've all heard harmonics from instruments since we were babies so the basic concept isn't too tough to think about when you listen to the following where the video author plays a single frequency versus a piano note (with it's harmonics we've all come to expect without knowing it):

It's not that different for electric waves. It's all waves.

I am still making measurement with my green wave meter to assess my dirty electricity situation. One of the items that I have identified as a major source is the JOOVV. It measures 1600 on the green wave meter, I can reduce it 4x by using one of the SAT IC Filters. This is certainly not as low as I would like to get but it’s better than nothing. I also used my trifield meter set on the electric scale ( I think this is equivalent to dirty electricity ). The readings were pretty low until I passed it over the bottom segment, where is pinner the meter. I am guessing that there are transformers or the light modulation circuit is located here. I took some aluminum screening and wrapped it around the lower segment and the dirty electricity disappeared. I also grounded the aluminum screen

### drezy — 2018-03-28T21:55:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-238157>

LOL. My main pipe \*does\* measure a field, but it's just gravitational.

My house's infrastructure pipes on the other hand measure clean. There is only a water input line and far as the pipes go unless you count septic. Both the water and septic are sustained on my property no utilities other than electricity.

There are advantages and disadvantages to this. When the septic pump (or aerobic aeration bubbler) dies here guess who puts on old clotes and fishes it out of the "grey" water tank... Better yet once a year or so when a grease layer slows my septic pipe it becomes like a game of clue, but instead of who killed Colonel Mustard in the dining room with a knife it's who's mal-absorbing their higher fat diet.

AM light is great in your eyes
Since your eyes are exposed you might as well expose your top
Since your top is exposed you might as well expose your bottom

Missing just one day per week is missing too much for me.

So far I haven't noticed a wood-o-meter difference with sporadic morning CT. That's my current N=1.
>
> [@kris90](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19157/) may have some interesting observations on this as well if he would like to chime in. I'd appreciate his observations.

Basically, CT + sun has significantly lowered my T levels. It makes sense, since T is loaded with deuterium, and CT and sun both lower deuterium. In Vermont this year, we learned that when deuterium is pressurized in the blood, it releases full spectrum UV light, including UV-C. Cold condenses deuterium, and so does the electromagnetic force of UV-A light.

So the way I see it, low T has a pathogenic, and non-pathogenic (or physiological) context. Low T in my circumstance means I'm producing a lot of this UV-C laser light which makes sense based on how I feel and how different I am from a true hypogonad individual. There is no negative impact on my libido or tent-pitching abilities whatsoever, so I just stopped focusing in on the numbers.

Mine used to float a more when I was vegan. I am guessing my liver also does not metabolize fats well because I feel bloated in the liver if I eat too much dense fats like pig fat. As a teen I used to be jaundiced a lot but fortunately that does not happen anymore. Processing resentment helped and not eating junk food which I stopped years ago helped too. I know I need to do some tests to get a reality check though.

I read it differently.

My translation of what they are saying:

If you stop consuming this water and go back to eating bonbons while binge watching netflix your numbers will tank faster than you can blink.

As a mitochondriac this is how I personally read it:

If you just used DDW and did not improve the environment that you got ill in, don't come bitch to us if your atouphagy and apoptosis still sucks.

And that's the thing for me. I've been quiet about this, but I don't think that DDW necessarily gives the signal for proper A&A (autophagy & apoptosis) which I suspect is often most needed. Buy you time and some repair, sure. But, the thought of remaining on DDW permanently does not make sense to me.

### JanSz — 2019-02-02T18:53:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-262126>

I am going to write it here, good to have on topic record of events.
May help someone.
[@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/)
It is about my 79yo friend in Poland who have advanced prostate cancer.
I am advising him (mostly) based on what I have learned from Jack.
He had prostate removed (I think) in 2005, since then he is on/of on hormonal therapy.
That therapy kills his otherwise very high total testosterone and that usually allows for his PSA to drop.
He had numerous radiations on his sacral bone where his cancer went to.
Since Jack started talking about deuterium/Boros, I am advising him to drink Qlarivia.
Initially he was against it, since he heard negative stuff about Boros.
Eventually he started drinkig diluted Qlarivia.
Lately, since Oct 2018 there is a talk about under-drinking.
I do not have good description from my friend, but it looks like for a time he was drinking 1liter a day of Qlarivia and no other water.
Eventually he was not thirsty, so it was easy to be on that amount of water.
After a time that caused him to stop peeing and very heavy constipation.
He stopped drinking Qlarivia all together and now drinks plain water.
He is irritated at me.

**It is apparent that hi is not making much water in matrix.**

He lives in one family town home, likely about 100 years old.
He live with his married daughter her husband and their children.
Expects to live 6 months to a year.
But that is recurring expectation.
He also have two sons living nearby with their families.

The only advice for him that I have left is to:
move to Playa Del Carmen
eat oysters and bacon
and for his remaining days stay whole day naked on the beach.
Do not even want to think about excessive nnEMF topic during long flights.

My friend speaks and writes English but barely.
I have tried to talk him into (conference) consultation with [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) over Skype.
It have not worked first time,
but I my renew this topic if Jack thinks that it could benefit him.

One more, possibly rather than the next anti-hormonal injection,
he should opt for castration (less chemicals).

...................

### Sue-UK — 2019-03-11T17:38:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-265121>

Thought this was interesting in relation to homocysteine.

"While the view that atherosclerosis is an inflammatory disease is gaining in popularity today \[149\], anti-inflammatory pharmaceutical drugs increase the risk to heart disease \[150\]. Elevated homocysteine is a strong risk factor for atherosclerosis, yet attempts to correct it have met with failure. Administration of B vitamin supplements in an attempt to reduce serum homocysteine has been effective, but did not result in any clinical improvement in cardiac disease risk \[151–153\]. All of these seemingly contradictory results can be explained if we assume the point of view that the inflammation is needed to induce superoxide production, which will allow sulfate production with homocysteine, H2S, and superoxide as substrates. Any attempt to reduce either the superoxide or the homocysteine levels will further impair the supply of sulfate to the capillary glycocalyx."

page 19 [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4456713/pdf/12976\_2015\_Article\_6.pdf](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4456713/pdf/12976_2015_Article_6.pdf)

Another homocysteine mention, this time in relation to aluminium.

_"These cardiovascular changes are likely related to aluminum’s impact on homocysteine. By inhibiting the methylation of homocysteine to methionine, aluminum ions cause blood homocysteine levels to rise. Acute homocysteine elevations will increase blood pressure. When this becomes chronic, the net effect is endothelial damage and atherosclerosis."_

I am also trying to heal all the wounds I got from having the Squamus cell skin cancer removed. I am also Treating the Imuran induced warts on my feet.

I am also working on my Bluteko breathing is that DDC had recommended. My controlled pause is still around 26 and my breathing rate is about eight breathe/min. ### JanSz — 2019-06-07T04:40:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-272179>

Pete
I have a hammer, looking for a nail. fishing

You suffer from CLIPPERS.

Chronic lymphocytic inflammation with pontine perivascular enhancement responsive to steroids (**CLIPPERS**) is a treatable inflammatory **disease** of the central nervous system. Specifically, it is a type of encephalomyelitis, which is a general term describing inflammation of the brain and spinal cord.
Mar 28, 2018

CLIPPERS tends to be a diagno-sis of exclusion, with patients often initially treated for stroke, encephalitis, multiple sclerosis or Wernicke encephalopathy.2
[https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/pdf/10.1177/1751143717719644](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/pdf/10.1177/1751143717719644)

[https://api.intechopen.com/chapter/pdf-preview/35745](https://api.intechopen.com/chapter/pdf-preview/35745)
Wernicke’s Encephalopathy (WE) is an underdiagnosed, potentially fatal, acute or subacute neurologic disorder caused by the impairment of thiamine (vitamin B1) -dependent enzymatic activity in susceptible brain cells.

\-----------------------------------------------
Vitamin B1 = thiamine
Supplemental thiamine is Benfothiamine
It works everywhere except it can't cross the brain barrier.
To cross brain barier use sulbuthiamine.
\------------------------------------------------

Are you using B1 supplementation or have you been using it?
If yes, what kind B1 have you used?

I have learned about sulbutiamine from [@DrEttinger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22307/)
Possibly he will have a time to chime in.

.........

### brookway17 — 2019-06-24T02:39:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/petes-optimal-journal.19169/post-273172>

I have been refining my red light/NIR treatments. I read a book about red light therapy written by Ari Witten which gave a lot of good advice about dosages, something that hard to find. I am now treating my deep tissues for 8 minutes Per surface.

I arrived at this treatment duration after looking at some Suggested dosing charts in the book. I had to calculate the time for the irradiance of the Joovv lite

I am giving my back nine minutes as this seems to help my sleep. I am also giving my balls eight minutes but only from one side, I usually illuminate them from the back. I have noticed that my libido has improved after a recently crashing.

I have also been trying to treat my lower brainstem, where the clippers is attacking my Pons. I am illuminating it from both sides for eight minutes through my ear region, which was recommended in the book.

Because the NI R only penetrates about 1.5 to 2 inches I think it is necessary to go at it from two sides.

For skin it was suggested to use a lower energy level. I calculated from these charts to use only two minutes per area. I am treating all my wounds from my Squamish cell skin cancer surgery. Also trying to treat all the damage from the years of steroids. It seems to be helping

\================
Speaking of your drinking 1.5L and trying to drink less than that.
Get yourself refractometer ($21.99)
When in doubt check
Urine Specific Gravity SG
As long as you read 1.035 or less, your kidneys should be fine, and you will have time to figure out what to do.

I am guessing that you should be able to benefit more than I do.
Mine breath-urine difference is less than 2.
Yours right at the beginning of drinking DDW was already
152.4-142.9=9.5
which I would kill for.
Boros wants 7-10
and you already have 9.5

Make sure that you do not get confused with misquotations of actual intent.
Drinking less does not mean that your body will have to deal with less water.
When all works as intended, you will
**pee as a horse.**
Otherwise, to say, you will pee more than what you drank.
But overall far less than the (5 - 6) liters.

**Again, overall, the guy who is selling you DDW will not make much money on you, because you will be drinking very little of his DDW.**

[https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B01MUBA92X/ref=ppx\_yo\_dt\_b\_search\_asin\_title?ie=UTF8&psc=1](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B01MUBA92X/ref=ppx_yo_dt_b_search_asin_title?ie=UTF8&psc=1)
